Drag to rotate: the original 2D sequence is rebuilt from 3D boxes, cylinders, cones, and lens spheres.
⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: horizontal track line representing the camera lateral translation axis (X-axis)
five side-by-side camera wireframe frustums showing a discrete sequence of lateral motion, with a black sphere at each apex
one active camera frustum highlighted dynamically as it translates along the horizontal track
three abstract 3D target points color-coded by depth: Near (red, Z=3), Mid (green, Z=7), Far (blue, Z=18)
projection rays from the three points to the active camera apex and extending to its virtual image plane
virtual image plane on the active camera displaying the projected 2D positions of the three points
2D velocity vector arrows (optical flow) drawn on the active image plane for each projected point
faint traces of projection paths showing how the projected points trace lines across the image plane during translation

interactions.label: Camera Position (X)
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates the physical transition of the camera along a lateral track, showing how viewpoint change alters projection angles

interactions.label: Target Depth (Z)
interactions.teaches: Modifies the depth of a test target point, revealing that closer objects experience larger angular sweeps than distant ones

interactions.label: Show Camera Sequence
interactions.teaches: Overlays the 5 discrete camera positions to match the classic textbook diagram of a parallel multi-camera setup

interactions.label: Show Flow Vectors
interactions.teaches: Enables the 2D apparent velocity vectors on the image plane, showing that image motion is opposite to camera translation and scaled by inverse depth (1/Z)

demo_steps.title: Lateral camera setup
demo_steps.narration: Welcome to lateral camera motion. Here we have a camera translating parallel to the image plane along the X-axis. Notice the 5 wireframe cameras representing discrete steps of this motion, with three abstract points at different depths in front of them: Near (red), Mid (green), and Far (blue).

demo_steps.title: The effect of camera translation
demo_steps.narration: As we translate the active camera to the right (X = 1.5), observe the projection rays. The red near point projects to a point that shifts rapidly across the virtual sensor, while the blue far point's projection barely shifts. This visual discrepancy is the parallax effect.

demo_steps.title: Visualizing the motion field
demo_steps.narration: Now let's enable the flow vectors. With the camera moving to the right, the apparent motion of the points on the image plane is to the left (the opposite direction). Notice how the length of the flow vectors is inversely proportional to depth: the closest point has the longest vector, and the farthest is nearly stationary.

demo_steps.title: Interrogating depth limits
demo_steps.narration: Let's change the depth of our target test point. Shrink its depth Z to 2. Notice how its projected flow vector grows massive — close objects fly across the field of view. Conversely, as we slide Z out to 20, its flow vector shrinks to almost zero, simulating infinitely distant objects like clouds.

plan.camera_suggestion: Elevated perspective view from the front-right, looking down at the multi-camera array and the 3D target points, allowing clear sight of both the spatial 3D layout and the active camera's 2D sensor projection.
plan.notes: Keep the styling highly abstract and clean: wireframe pyramids for camera frustums, a simple black sphere at each camera apex, and simple glowing spheres for the target points. Project the 3D points onto a small semi-transparent rectangular plane representing the camera sensor behind the apex.

elements: translating camera model (frustum, sensor plane, and lens center)
horizontal path line indicating camera lateral motion
three scene objects at distinct depths: a foreground tree (Z=3), a midground house (Z=7), and a distant background cloud (Z=20)
projection rays from each object through the lens center to the sensor plane
sensor plane visualization showing the projected 2D positions of the three objects
2D velocity vectors (optical flow arrows) drawn on the sensor plane corresponding to each object's apparent motion
coordinate axes (X, Y, Z) aligned with the camera's coordinate frame

interactions.label: Camera Position (X)
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates how lateral camera translation shifts the projected position of 3D points on the sensor plane, visually showing the geometric basis of parallax

interactions.label: Camera Velocity (Vx)
interactions.teaches: Controls the continuous lateral movement speed of the camera, which determines the magnitude and direction of the optical flow vectors on the sensor

interactions.label: Focal Length (f)
interactions.teaches: Shows how scaling the focal length scales the apparent image plane velocity proportionally according to the motion field equation

interactions.label: Show Flow Vectors
interactions.teaches: Overlays 2D motion vectors on the sensor plane to highlight how apparent velocity is inversely proportional to depth (-f * Vx / Z)

demo_steps.title: Lateral Camera Motion Setup
demo_steps.narration: Welcome to lateral camera motion. We have a camera pointing down the Z-axis that can translate along the horizontal X-axis. Notice three objects in the scene at different depths: a close-up tree, a midground house, and a far-away cloud.

demo_steps.title: Observing Parallax Geometrically
demo_steps.narration: As we translate the camera to the right (X = 2.0), observe how the projection rays cross. The close-up tree sweeps rapidly across the camera's sensor, while the distant cloud barely shifts. This discrepancy in apparent movement is called parallax.

demo_steps.title: Visualizing Optical Flow
demo_steps.narration: Let's set the camera in motion with a constant velocity Vx = 1.0 and enable the flow vectors. Notice the green arrows on the camera sensor: they point to the left, opposite to the camera's rightward motion, as described by the negative sign in the velocity equation.

demo_steps.title: The Inverse Depth (1/Z) Law
demo_steps.narration: Look closely at the length of the flow vectors on the sensor. The tree (Z=3) has a huge velocity vector, the house (Z=7) has a medium vector, and the distant cloud (Z=20) has a vector near zero. Because flow magnitude scales with 1/Z, closer objects appear to fly by while distant objects look static.

demo_steps.title: Focal Length Magnification
demo_steps.narration: Finally, let's double the focal length 'f' to 3.0. This moves the sensor plane farther from the lens center, magnifying the projected scene. Notice that all the flow vectors double in length, demonstrating the linear scaling with 'f' in the motion field equation.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Elevated perspective view from the side-front, looking down at the camera and the scene objects, allowing the user to simultaneously see the 3D spatial layout and the 2D sensor plane on the back of the camera.
interactionPlan.notes: Render a picture-in-picture 2D view or float a semi-transparent screen behind the camera body to clearly show the sensor-plane projections. Color-code the three objects (e.g., green tree, red house, blue cloud) and match these colors on the sensor plane projections and flow vectors to make the visual link instantaneous.

attempts.html:
Lateral camera motion
Drag the sliders to see parallax and optical flow.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The source figure shows 5 static cameras in a horizontal sequence, whereas the generated scene shows a single animated/interactive camera.
The source figure contains no background objects (tree, house, cloud) or projection lines, which are heavily featured in the generated scene.
The source figure has no text labels or coordinate axes, while the generated scene is highly annotated with labels and formulas.
The cameras in the source figure are simple wireframe frustums with a black dot at the center, whereas the generated camera has a box body, axes, and a colored sensor plane.
evaluation.notes: The generated figure is an excellent, highly interactive pedagogical demo of lateral camera motion, but it deviates significantly from the extremely simple 2D schematic layout of the source figure.
action_items: Simplify the scene by removing the extra 3D environment elements (tree, house, cloud) to match the abstract nature of the textbook illustration if strict faithfulness is desired.
Recreate the 5-camera sequence side-by-side as shown in the source figure, perhaps allowing the user to scrub a slider to highlight/animate through the sequence.
Reduce label clutter to match the clean, label-free style of the original diagram.
Adjust the camera's visual representation to exactly match the wireframe pyramid/frustum style with a black sphere at the apex.

feedback.rationale: The planner hallucinated an overly complex 3D scene with trees, houses, and labels, failing to capture the simple structure of five sequential static cameras shown in the source figure.
action_items: Simplify the scene by removing the extra 3D environment elements (tree, house, cloud) to match the abstract nature of the textbook illustration if strict faithfulness is desired.
Recreate the 5-camera sequence side-by-side as shown in the source figure, perhaps allowing the user to scrub a slider to highlight/animate through the sequence.
Reduce label clutter to match the clean, label-free style of the original diagram.
Adjust the camera's visual representation to exactly match the wireframe pyramid/frustum style with a black sphere at the apex.
actionItems: Simplify the scene by removing the extra 3D environment elements (tree, house, cloud) to match the abstract nature of the textbook illustration if strict faithfulness is desired.
Recreate the 5-camera sequence side-by-side as shown in the source figure, perhaps allowing the user to scrub a slider to highlight/animate through the sequence.
Reduce label clutter to match the clean, label-free style of the original diagram.
Adjust the camera's visual representation to exactly match the wireframe pyramid/frustum style with a black sphere at the apex.

attempts.html:
Repeated camera positions with red arrows showing rightward translation.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The final red arrow on the far right is missing in the generated version
The elements are entirely flat 2D planes instead of 3D meshes
OrbitControls rotation is disabled, preventing 3D exploration of the camera translation path
evaluation.notes: The schematic is visually very faithful to the 2D original but lacks 3D depth and interactive rotation.
action_items: Recreate the camera icons as actual 3D wireframe or solid models
Enable OrbitControls rotation to allow viewing the translation sequence from perspective angles
Add the missing final red arrow to the right of the fifth camera
Add 3D text labels to indicate translation step coordinates or distance markers

feedback.rationale: The overall layout and concept are faithful, but the generator needs to execute it with actual 3D primitives and enable camera rotation instead of flattening it to 2D with locked controls.
action_items: Recreate the camera icons as actual 3D wireframe or solid models
Enable OrbitControls rotation to allow viewing the translation sequence from perspective angles
Add the missing final red arrow to the right of the fifth camera
Add 3D text labels to indicate translation step coordinates or distance markers

attempts.html:
Drag to rotate: the original 2D sequence is rebuilt from 3D boxes, cylinders, cones, and lens spheres.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The original image features 6 red arrows (including one leading in from the left and one pointing out to the right), while the generated figure only contains 5.

The camera symbol in the original is a simpler 2D-like projection (single flat rectangle with a cross and lines converging to a point), whereas the generated figure models it with a full double-planed 3D box frustum, making it look busier in Front View.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive extension of a simple 2D textbook sequence into an adjustable 3D workspace, though the camera wireframes are slightly more complex than the original flat drawing.
action_items: Add the sixth red arrow at the far right of the sequence to match the original layout's entry and exit arrows.

Simplify the camera icon wireframe geometry in 'Front view' to more closely resemble the single-plane cross representation of the original diagram if possible, or make the extra depth lines thinner/more transparent.
Ensure the camera wireframe lines converge perfectly to the center of the lens sphere in the Front view projection.

feedback.rationale: With an overall score of 4.6, no failure modes, and only minor cosmetic suggestions for the camera wireframe and an extra arrow, the figure is of excellent quality and ready to pass.
action_items: Add the sixth red arrow at the far right of the sequence to match the original layout's entry and exit arrows.

Ensure the camera wireframe lines converge perfectly to the center of the lens sphere in the Front view projection.
